applied_optimizations.name: Triton kernel with block processing
applied_optimizations.description: Implemented a Triton kernel for 3D max pooling that processes 256 output elements per program using tl.arange for vectorized processing. Key optimizations: (1) Use block processing with BLOCK_SIZE=256 to amortize kernel launch overhead, (2) Use tl.arange for parallel element processing within each block, (3) Manually unroll the 3x3x3 kernel window (27 positions) with proper bounds checking, (4) Use explicit strides for pointer arithmetic to avoid overflow issues.

attempted_but_failed.name: torch.compile on MaxPool3d
attempted_but_failed.reason: Compilation overhead made it slower (8.36ms vs 3.44ms baseline)

attempted_but_failed.name: Single-element Triton kernel
attempted_but_failed.reason: Integer overflow in pointer arithmetic for large tensors
attempted_but_failed.error: 32-bit overflow when computing offsets for (16,32,128,128,128) tensor

attempted_but_failed.name: Triton kernel with runtime loops
attempted_but_failed.reason: Triton doesn't support runtime values in for loops

notes: 3D max pooling is memory-bound - the Triton kernel achieves 1.77x speedup by fusing the read-compute-write into a single kernel
Key insight: Use tl.arange for block processing instead of scalar per-element kernels
Triton on ROCm requires explicit stride passing and careful handling of integer types for large tensors
The kernel processes 256 elements per program, each element reading up to 27 input values (3x3x3 window with dilation=3)

attempted_but_failed.name: trial_4
attempted_but_failed.reason: Three consecutive trials (2, 3, 4) produced no output. With only ~22 minutes remaining and a pattern of agent failures, further trials are unlikely to succeed. The best result of 67.70 (1.77x speedup

global_notes: Successfully implemented Triton kernel for 3D max pooling with 1.77x speedup over PyTorch reference
